02_snakemake_CO2.run_1: Snakemake calculates a unique SHA-256 hash for each job by capturing its code, parameters, raw input files, software environment, and the hashes of its dependencies. This recursive hashing principle, similar to blockchains, allows the system to store and look up output files in a central local or remote cache. If a matching hash is found in a subsequent run or a different workflow, Snakemake can skip execution and retrieve the results directly from the cache.
02_snakemake_CO2.run_2: Snakemake calculates a unique SHA-256 hash for each job by recursively incorporating the hashes of its dependencies, code, parameters, and software environment, mirroring the hashing principle used in blockchains. This hash unambiguously identifies how an output file is generated, allowing Snakemake to store and look up results in a central cache. Consequently, subsequent runs of the same job in different workflows can skip execution and directly retrieve the output files from the cache, saving both computation time and disk space.
02_snakemake_CO2.run_3: Snakemake calculates a unique hash for each job based on its code, parameters, input files, software environment, and the hashes of its dependencies. Consequently, subsequent runs of the same job in different workflows or by different users can skip execution and directly use the cached output files.

02_snakemake_TE3.run_1: Snakemake optimizes job scheduling by solving a mixed integer linear program (MILP) that prioritizes high-priority jobs, maximizes parallelization, and ensures the rapid deletion of temporary files. To minimize job submission overhead in cluster or cloud environments, Snakemake utilizes graph partitioning to group connected subgraphs of the DAG into single "group jobs." These partitioned subgraphs are submitted together to the underlying queuing system, reducing the delays and costs associated with submitting many short-running jobs individually.

For dispersion, the framework fits a trend line to gene-wise maximum likelihood estimates and shrinks individual dispersions toward this trend to obtain final maximum a posteriori (MAP) values. For fold change estimation, it applies a zero-centered normal prior to shrink logarithmic fold change (LFC) estimates toward zero, with stronger shrinkage for genes with lower counts or higher dispersion where information is limited.

This process stabilizes noisy estimates and prevents the exaggeration of effect sizes for genes with limited information, leading to more reliable differential expression analysis.
